_notes.purpose: Suggested name chips for each Naming Moment. Displayed as tappable chips after the description paragraph. Progression: functional (what it does) → evocative (how it feels) → personal (a name). Always followed by an open text field. User can select any chip or type their own. The chosen name becomes the part's displayName.
_notes.chip_count: 5-6 chips per cluster. Final chip is always a common human name to open the 'personal' register.
_notes._on_revision: Chips are cheap to change. After running the assessment yourself and with clients, revise any that don't land. The functional names should feel recognizable. The evocative names should feel slightly surprising but true. The personal names should feel warm and human.

clusterA.label: Standards & Effort

chips.note: Direct, neutral — describes what the part does. Good anchor for people who don't yet relate to the part as a being.
More kinetic — suggests momentum and pressure. Works well for people who feel this part as energy or push.
Implies constant vigilance — a part that never fully rests.
For people who experience this part as harsh or relentless — names the severity without shame.
Familiar idiom that captures the demanding quality.
Gender-neutral human name — opens the door to relating to this part as a person, not just a function.

clusterA.open_field_prompt: Or give it your own name

clusterB.label: Relief & Escape

chips.note: Mechanical — captures the release function without judgment. Good for people who experience this part as involuntary.
Slightly playful — names the skill without shaming it. Works well when the person has some affection for this part.
Neutral, accurate, emphasizes the intent not the behavior.
Suggests what happens when this part takes over — things loosen, come undone.

chips.value: The One Who Needs a Break
chips.note: Humanizing — reminds the user this part is exhausted, not just impulsive.
Gender-neutral, approachable — invites the user to see this part as a specific being with a history.

clusterB.open_field_prompt: Or give it your own name
clusterB._note: No chips here should reference the specific behavior (drinking, eating, spending). Keep it behavior-agnostic.

clusterC.label: Connection & Relationships

chips.note: Neutral, accurate — describes the core move without judgment.
Familiar term that most people immediately recognize in themselves.
More positive framing — good when the person experiences this part as genuinely caring, not just anxious.
For the part that monitors more than it accommodates — the watchful, alert quality.

chips.value: The One Who Holds It Together
chips.note: Captures the exhausted, quietly heroic quality many people feel about this part.
Gender-neutral, grounded — brings warmth to a part that often feels invisible.

clusterC.open_field_prompt: Or give it your own name

clusterD_critic.label: The Voice Inside

chips.note: Simple, direct — most people immediately recognize it. Sometimes the most honest name is the right name.
Slightly more formal — works well for people who experience this part as issuing verdicts.
For people whose inner critic is punishing more than coaching — names the severity directly.
Implies surveillance and control — good for people who feel this part as omnipresent.
Sometimes the simplest name is the most accurate. Good for people who don't yet have distance from this part.
Everyday human name — deliberately ordinary to contrast with the weight of the part's role.

clusterD_critic.open_field_prompt: Or give it your own name

micro_capture._note: Displayed after each naming confirmation. Free text only — no chips. Stored in assessment_naming_moments.feel_towards. Never scored, never shown back to user as analysis.
micro_capture.prompt_template: How do you feel toward [chosen_name] right now?
micro_capture.placeholder: Curious, grateful, tired, wary, something else entirely...
micro_capture.subtext: There's no right answer. Just notice.
